title: Who is Nipun
text: Nipun Sujesh is an AI engineer and builder from Kerala, India. Final-year B.E. student in Artificial Intelligence and Data Science at Kathir College of Engineering (Anna University), GPA 8.24/10, graduating 2026. He builds AI products end to end: models, backends, and interfaces. Currently building Uni-Verse and working as an AI Workflow Automation Engineer Intern at impress.ai. Portfolio: nipun.space.

title: Current focus
text: Most of Nipun's time goes to building Uni-Verse, an honest, on-the-record platform for college life in India. He also works at impress.ai on LLM and RAG powered workflow automation, and ships side projects constantly. He is open to Generative AI Engineer roles for 2026.

title: Experience at impress.ai

Ships through a three-branch deployment flow using Claude workflows, Google Apps Script, and the Slack API.

title: Uni-Verse
text: Uni-Verse is the platform Nipun is currently building: an honest, on-the-record record of what India's colleges are actually like, written by verified students. Like Glassdoor but for campuses. The core rule is anonymous but verified: students prove they study there, then their identity is locked away. It has a Verity Score (0 to 1000) that colleges can only earn, not buy. Currently at the landing page and waitlist stage at uni-verse.co.in, collecting the first thousand Founding Voices; the full platform is in active development.

title: Flagship build
text: Serenity is Nipun's most complete production build to date: a full-stack multimodal AI mental-health assistant with authentication, a four-tier memory system, a fine-tuned XLM-RoBERTa emotion classifier at 81.3% accuracy, 95% safety recall on crisis detection, and a research paper submitted to ICRAIA 2025. Deployed and live at serenity.nipun.space. When judging his deepest or most impressive shipped work, Serenity is the flagship; Uni-Verse is the most ambitious but is still early-stage.

title: About this website
text: This portfolio, nipun.os, is a terminal-themed site designed and coded by Nipun with React 19, Vite, Tailwind, Framer Motion and Three.js. Navigation works through the command bar at the bottom: type / for commands, or just type a question to ask LucBot, the site's assistant. LucBot answers using hybrid retrieval (BM25 plus embeddings) over the site's own content through a serverless proxy speaking the new HTTP QUERY method (RFC 10008) with POST fallback, and can suggest pages with clickable buttons.

title: Giving an AI assistant a memory that doesn't rot — Giving an AI assistant a memory that doesn't rot
text: Most AI assistants have the memory of a goldfish with a notebook. They either remember nothing past the context window, or they dump every message into a vector store and call it memory. I tried the second approach first. It rots. Serenity is a mental health assistant I built as my capstone. In that domain, memory is not a nice-to-have. If someone tells you on Monday that exams are crushing them, and on Thursday you ask how work is going, you have lost them. Worse, you might have hurt them.

title: Giving an AI assistant a memory that doesn't rot — Why one vector store fails
text: Naive retrieval treats every memory as equal. It isn't. What someone said ten minutes ago, what they said last week, what stays true about them, and what you concluded across many sessions are four different kinds of information. Stuffing them into one index gives you noise dressed up as recall. So Serenity runs four tiers. Working memory holds the live conversation. Session memory summarizes each conversation once it ends. Long-term memory holds durable facts: triggers, preferences, patterns. And insight memory holds conclusions the system forms slowly, across sessions. Each tier has its own write rules, its own decay, and its own retrieval weight.

title: Giving an AI assistant a memory that doesn't rot — The part that surprised me
text: The hard part was not retrieval. It was deciding what gets to be remembered. Everything cannot be a memory or nothing is. Session summaries go through a filter that asks a blunt question: would a good therapist write this down? Most of a conversation does not survive that question. For the emotion layer, I fine-tuned XLM-RoBERTa on conversational data. It reached 81.3% emotion classification accuracy at around 680 ms median response. The crisis detection pipeline hit 95% safety recall, which was the number I actually cared about. Missing a crisis is not an acceptable failure mode, so the system is tuned to be paranoid there and casual everywhere else. The paper on this is submitted to ICRAIA

title: Giving an AI assistant a memory that doesn't rot — The part that surprised me
text: 2025. The live build is at serenity.nipun.space. It is not perfect. But it remembers Thursday what you told it Monday, and it turns out that alone puts you ahead of most of the field.

title: Why I'm building Uni-Verse — Why I'm building Uni-Verse
text: Every year, lakhs of students in India pick a college based on ads, paid rankings, and a campus tour that was staged down to the flower pots. Then the fees are paid, and they find out what the place is actually like. By then it is too late to matter. I am a final-year student. I watched this happen around me, and to me. The information students needed existed the whole time. It lived in senior group chats, hostel corridors, and late-night conversations. It just never sat anywhere out in the open, anywhere a college could not pay to bury it.

title: Why I'm building Uni-Verse — The honest record
text: Uni-Verse is my answer: an honest record of what India's colleges are actually like, written by verified students living there. Think Glassdoor, but for campuses. What a college advertises sits next to what its own students report. One of those can be bought. The other cannot. The whole platform runs on one rule: anonymous, but verified. You prove you study there with your college identity. Then that identity is locked away, and nothing you post can be traced back to you. Not by your college, not by other students, not by me.

title: Why I'm building Uni-Verse — Why anonymity alone always dies
text: Yik Yak, Secret, Sarahah. Every anonymous app dies the same death, because anonymity without proof is just noise with a mask on. Uni-Verse flips the order. Prove you belong first. Then disappear. What remains is a record where every voice is real and no voice is exposed. There is also a score. The Verity Score, zero to a thousand, computed from verified student reports and how a college responds to them. A college cannot buy it up. They can only earn it. That is the point. The landing page is live at uni-verse.co.in and the waitlist is open. I am collecting the first thousand students, the Founding Voices, before full rollout. If you are a student in India, that spot is yours to claim.

title: Agents that build their own tools — Agents that build their own tools
text: The standard way to build an agent looks like this: you write ten tool definitions by hand, wire them to functions, and pray the model picks the right one. It works. It also means every schema change is a code change, and your agent only knows what you remembered to teach it. For the Google Gen AI Academy hackathon, I wanted to see how far the opposite approach could go. PRAGATI ships with zero hardcoded tools.

title: Agents that build their own tools — How it works
text: A Root Orchestrator agent connects to AlloyDB and introspects the schema at runtime. Tables, columns, types, relationships. From that, it generates MCP tool definitions on the fly, registers them, and routes natural language queries to tools that did not exist a second earlier. Change the database and the toolset changes with it. No redeploy. Google ADK for the agent framework. MCP Toolbox for dynamic tool generation. AlloyDB with pgvector for storage and similarity search. Gemini Flash on Vertex AI for the reasoning layer. FastAPI and Docker on Cloud Run for serving We ran it over 7,894 rows of public health data. Ask it something in plain English and you can watch it decide which tool it n

title: Agents that build their own tools — How it works
text: eeds, notice the tool does not exist, build it, and then use it. The first time that loop closed end to end, I sat there grinning at a terminal for a solid minute.

title: Agents that build their own tools — What I took away
text: Dynamic tooling is not free. You trade compile-time safety for runtime flexibility, so validation and guardrails have to move into the loop. The agent needs to check its own generated tools before trusting them, which feels strange to write and stranger to debug. But the direction feels right. MCP gives tools a standard shape, and once tools have a standard shape, generating them stops being exotic. I think a lot of agent infrastructure ends up here: less hand-wiring, more systems that read their environment and assemble what they need. PRAGATI is on GitHub if you want to dig through it.

title: What RAG looks like once it has a job — What RAG looks like once it has a job
text: Building a RAG demo takes an afternoon. Embed some documents, wire up retrieval, pass chunks to a model. It answers questions and everyone claps. Then you give it a real job, where real people act on its answers every day, and a different project begins. I spend my days building LLM and RAG powered workflow automation at an AI company. Most of what I have learned is not in the tutorials.

title: What RAG looks like once it has a job — Retrieval is the product
text: When a RAG system is wrong, the model usually is not the problem. Retrieval is. Garbage chunks in, confident nonsense out. The unglamorous work is upstream: how documents get split, what metadata rides along with each chunk, when to filter before searching instead of after. Chunking strategy affects answer quality more than model choice. Nobody wants to hear that, because chunking is boring. It is also the job.

title: What RAG looks like once it has a job — Evals before vibes
text: In a demo you check five answers by eye and ship. In production you need a set of questions with known answers, run on every change. Not hundreds to start. Even twenty good ones catch most regressions. Without them, every prompt tweak is a coin flip you cannot see landing.

title: What RAG looks like once it has a job — Boring beats clever
text: My rule now: the automation that survives is the one a non-engineer can trust without understanding. That means predictable failure modes, answers that cite where they came from, and a system that says it does not know instead of improvising. A slightly dumber pipeline that fails loudly beats a clever one that fails quietly. Every time. None of this is glamorous. All of it is the difference between a demo and a tool people rely on. If you are taking RAG to production, spend your effort in this order: retrieval quality, evals, failure behavior, and only then the model.

title: MCP quietly became the plug — MCP quietly became the plug
text: Two years ago, connecting an LLM to your tools meant picking a framework and marrying it. Every integration was bespoke. Your database connector worked in one stack and nowhere else, and switching frameworks meant rewriting everything that touched the outside world. Then the Model Context Protocol showed up and did something unfashionable. It did not try to be an agent framework, an orchestrator, or a platform. It just standardized the plug: a common shape for how a model discovers tools, calls them, and reads what comes back.

title: MCP quietly became the plug — Boring is a strategy
text: USB did not win because it was exciting. It won because it ended the drawer full of proprietary cables. MCP is doing the same thing for AI tooling. Write a server once, and any MCP-speaking client can use it. The connector outlives the framework you happened to write it in, which quietly changes where the value sits. I feel this daily. On PRAGATI, my agent generates MCP tool definitions at runtime, which is only possible because tools have a standard shape to generate into. At work, connectors we build are useful beyond the stack they were born in. And half my own development setup is MCP servers talking to coding agents.

title: MCP quietly became the plug — Where I think this goes
text: Frameworks will keep churning. Standards stick around. My bet is that in a few years nobody remembers which agent framework you used in 2025, the same way nobody asks which HTTP library a website was built with. The interesting question moves up a level: what tools does your system expose, and how well do they compose. If you are building anything agent-shaped, expose your capability as an MCP server first and integrate frameworks second. The cable drawer is closed. Good riddance.

title: Serenity
text: Serenity (PRODUCTION, 2025). Stack: Python · React · FastAPI · Ollama. Live at https://serenity.nipun.space. Serenity is an AI-powered mental-health companion, built as a final-year BTech (AI & Data Science) project. It combines a layered memory system, adaptive personality, real-time emotion detection, and crisis-safe response logic into a single full-stack application, deployed and actively in developmen

title: Serenity — README.md
text: A quieter place for your mind. Serenity is an AI-powered mental-health companion, built as a final-year BTech (AI & Data Science) project. It combines a layered memory system, adaptive personality, real-time emotion detection, and crisis-safe response logic into a single full-stack application, deployed and actively in development.

title: Serenity — What it does
text: Most chatbots forget you the moment a conversation ends. Serenity doesn't. Every interaction is stored across four memory tiers (short-term context, semantic vector memory, a 30-day emotional profile, and pattern-based meta-reflections), so the assistant builds a genuine understanding of the user over time. Alongside memory, it runs a real-time emotion pipeline on every message, adapts its personality to what the user needs in that moment, and routes any input flagged as a crisis through a hardened response path before the LLM ever sees it.

title: Serenity — Architecture
text: The system is divided into five layers, each swappable at config level. Frontend: React 19 SPA (Vite, CSS Modules, Framer Motion, GSAP). Backend: FastAPI, async SQLAlchemy 2.0, pluggable Emotion / LLM / Crisis engines. Database: PostgreSQL 15 (Supabase), 10 tables, Alembic. AI / ML: keyword (~65%) + XLNet (~88%) emotion, Ollama Cloud LLM with fallback. Infra: Docker Compose dev; Vercel + Railway + Supabase prod

title: Serenity — Memory System
text: The core technical contribution. Each chat message triggers a four-tier memory bundle before the LLM prompt is assembled. Short-term: Cached conversation summaries (session). Semantic: 384-dim vector similarity search (sentence-transformers). Emotional Profile: 30-day rolling aggregation (12h cache). Meta-Reflections: Journal + conversation pattern synthesis (2d cache) The LLM receives all four tiers as structured context, so responses reference past states, recurring themes and long-term patterns, not just the last few messages.

title: Serenity — Crisis Detection
text: Crisis assessment runs first, before any LLM call. A conservative keyword engine (high precision over recall) classifies severity and returns resources (988, 741741, 911) on trigger; the turn terminates there and nothing reaches the LLM. Events are logged with severity and keywords.

title: Serenity — Chat Flow
text: Input → crisis check → ConversationService → EmotionService → ContextManager → 4-tier memory bundle → LLM prompt → Ollama → save → background tasks → typewriter display.

title: Serenity — Feature Status
text: Shipped: real-time streaming chat, four-mode personality, four-tier memory, crisis detection, journal + analytics, JWT auth, Docker deploy. In progress: XLNet as the default emotion engine, the Insights visualisations, and mobile polish. Planned: voice input, wearable integration, and a therapist portal.

title: Serenity — Running Locally
text: Prerequisites: Docker and Docker Compose.

title: Serenity — API
text: POST /api/auth/signup: Register. POST /api/auth/login: Login, returns JWT. POST /api/chat: Send message, returns AI response. GET /api/conversations: List user conversations. GET /api/emotions/analytics: Emotion trend data. GET /api/journal: Journal entries. POST /api/journal: Create journal entry. GET /health: Health check

title: Serenity — Status
text: Deployed and functional, actively in development. License: AGPL-3.0.

title: Serenity — features.md
text: Most chatbots forget you the moment a conversation ends. Serenity doesn't. Every interaction is stored across four memory tiers, so the assistant builds a genuine understanding of the user over time rather than starting cold each session. Four-tier memory: short-term context, semantic vector memory, a 30-day emotional profile, and pattern-based meta-reflections. Real-time emotion pipeline that runs on every message. Adaptive personality (four modes) that shifts to what the user needs in the moment. Crisis inputs routed through a hardened response path before the LLM ever sees them. Journal with AI extraction, emotion analytics dashboard, streaming (SSE) chat

title: Serenity — architecture.md
text: The system is divided into five layers. Each is swappable at config level, so engines can be replaced without touching service code. Frontend: React 19 SPA with Vite, CSS Modules, Framer Motion and GSAP. Pages: CheckIn (chat), Journal, Insights, Meditate, Profile. State via context providers with localStorage persistence.. Backend: FastAPI with async SQLAlchemy 2.0. AI logic lives in a pluggable engine system (EmotionEngine, LLMEngine, CrisisEngine), swappable at config level. Background tasks run non-blocking via FastAPI BackgroundTasks.. Database: PostgreSQL 15 on Supabase. 10 tables: users, conversations, messages, emotion logs, journal entries, crisis events, semantic memories, emotional

title: Serenity — architecture.md
text: profiles, meta-reflections, context cache. Migrations via Alembic.. AI / ML: Keyword emotion engine (~65%, no GPU). Optional XLNet (~88%) and Ollama-based detection are pluggable. LLM via Ollama Cloud (gpt-oss:120b) with a template fallback chain.. Infrastructure: Docker Compose for local dev. Production across Vercel (frontend), Railway (backend), Supabase (PostgreSQL).

title: Serenity — memory.md
text: The core technical contribution. Each chat message triggers a four-tier memory bundle before the LLM prompt is assembled. Short-term: Cached conversation summaries (session TTL). Semantic: 384-dim vector similarity search via sentence-transformers. Emotional Profile: 30-day rolling emotion aggregation (12h cache). Meta-Reflections: Journal + conversation pattern synthesis (2d cache) The LLM receives all four tiers as structured context, allowing responses that reference past emotional states, recurring themes and long-term patterns, not just the last few messages.

title: Serenity — crisis.md
text: Crisis assessment runs as the first step in every request, before any LLM call. It uses a keyword-based engine, intentionally conservative (high precision over recall), that classifies severity. If triggered, it immediately returns appropriate resources (988, 741741, 911) and the conversation terminates at that point; no LLM response is generated. Crisis events are logged to a dedicated table with severity, detected keywords and acknowledgement state.

title: Serenity — chatflow.md
text: Full pipeline, from user input to the typewriter display: User input (CheckIn, ChatContext, api.js validation). Crisis assessment (keyword match, severity, critical path). ConversationService, then EmotionService (keyword engine, optional XLNet). ContextManager assembles the 4-tier memory bundle. LLM prompt assembly → Ollama Cloud. Save to DB → non-blocking background tasks → typewriter display

title: Serenity — api.md
text: REST endpoints exposed by the FastAPI backend. POST /api/auth/signup: Register. POST /api/auth/login: Login, returns JWT. POST /api/chat: Send message, returns AI response. GET /api/conversations: List user conversations. GET /api/emotions/analytics: Emotion trend data. GET /api/journal: Journal entries. POST /api/journal: Create journal entry. GET /health: Health check

title: Pragati — README.md

title: Pragati — What makes it unique
text: Most AI agents are hand-coded with fixed tools. PRAGATI self-assembles its own MCP tools at runtime. Add a table to AlloyDB, restart, and it has a new tool, with zero code changes.

title: Pragati — How it boots
text: Cartographer introspects the AlloyDB schema, no hardcoded table names. Forge generates MCP Toolbox tool definitions from the discovered schema. Root Orchestrator (ADK agent) routes natural-language queries to the right tool. Gemini Flash synthesizes a natural-language answer from the raw data

text: At boot, the orchestrator wires its own tools from the live database schema, then routes each query to the right one.

title: Guardia
text: Guardia (PRODUCTION, 2026). Stack: YOLOv8-pose · Python · Android. Guardia watches over people who live alone using pose estimation and acts fast when something goes wrong. A final-year project.

title: Guardia — README.md
text: Real-time fall detection for elderly and solo-living safety. Guardia watches over people who live alone using pose estimation and acts fast when something goes wrong. A final-year project.

title: Guardia — What it does
text: Runs on a standard webcam, processing video in real time. On a fall: speaks an "Are you okay?" prompt, then an alarm if there's no response. Sends SMS and WhatsApp alerts to an emergency contact. Uploads a snapshot of the fall to cloud storage. Fall to SMS delivery in about 12 seconds

title: Guardia — how-it-works.md
text: Built on YOLOv8n-pose (pre-trained skeleton detection) with multi-signal heuristics layered on top, rather than a custom model. Velocity spike: sudden downward acceleration of the body centre. Horizontal posture: bounding-box aspect ratio flips when someone falls. Spine angle: skeleton orientation crosses toward horizontal. A fall is flagged only when 2 of the 3 signals trigger, cutting false positives. Per-person tracking, a 3.5s grace period, and a raise-hand self-cancel

title: Guardia — results.md
text: Tested on 75 clips across 7 categories with 3 subjects. Accuracy: 91.0%. Test set: 75 clips · 7 categories · 3 subjects. Latency: ~12s fall to SMS

title: Fake News Classifier
text: Fake News Classifier (BUILT, 2026). Stack: Python · scikit-learn · NLP. A clear, interpretable pipeline for binary fake-vs-factual news classification using classical NLP and linear models, focused on fundamentals over complex architectures. News articles with title, text, date, and a fake/factual label. A 70/30 train-test split.

title: Fake News Classifier — README.md
text: Fake-news detection with classical NLP. A clear, interpretable pipeline for binary fake-vs-factual news classification using classical NLP and linear models, focused on fundamentals over complex architectures.

title: Fake News Classifier — Pipeline
text: Text preprocessing. Feature extraction: Bag-of-Words / TF-IDF. Linear classifiers: Logistic Regression vs Linear SVM. Standard evaluation metrics

title: Fake News Classifier — Dataset
text: News articles with title, text, date, and a fake/factual label. A 70/30 train-test split.

title: Uni-Verse
text: Uni-Verse (CURRENT, 2026). Stack: Next.js · Supabase. Live at https://uni-verse.co.in. Uni-Verse is what I'm building now: verified student voices about colleges, kept public and on the record. Actively in development. Currently building. More detail as it ships.
